title: How to Use Intuition When Making Career Changes
meta_description: Trust what you already know. Your intuition is real. This article shows how to use it for career changes without the fluff.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Career Changes
og_description: Trust what you already know. Your intuition is real. This article shows how to use it for career changes without the fluff.
body:
The Job That's Killing You Softly

You're sitting in your car in the parking lot. You've been sitting here for eleven minutes after your commute ended. The engine is off. The radio is silent. You're staring at the building where you'll spend the next nine hours, and something in your chest is saying no. Not a loud no. Not a dramatic no. It's more like a low hum of wrongness that you've been ignoring for two years.

You've been told your whole life that this feeling doesn't matter. That feelings are unreliable. That you need to be practical. That you should be grateful you have a job at all. So you push through. You get out of the car. You walk through the doors. You do your work. And a little piece of you dies every day. Nothing dramatic. Just a slow leak of your aliveness.

I'm Kalesh, and I teach people how to trust what they already know but were taught to ignore. If you're reading this, you're probably someone who feels things deeply. You pick up on the energy of rooms. You know when something's off before anyone says a word. And you've been told your whole life that something is wrong with you because of it. Nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. The problem isn't that you feel too much. The problem is that you've been trained to distrust what you feel.

Career changes are where this distrust shows up most painfully. Because career decisions feel high-stakes. You have bills. You have rent. You have people counting on you. And the idea of trusting a "gut feeling" when your livelihood is on the line can feel like lunacy. But here's the thing - your intuition isn't a vague, mystical whisper from the universe. It's your nervous system sending you real-time data about whether an environment is safe for you to thrive in. And right now, your system is screaming that you're in the wrong place.

Why Your Rational Brain Will Lie to You

Your rational brain is great at solving math problems. It's terrible at making life decisions. Here's why. Your rational brain runs on fear. It's wired for survival, not fulfillment. When you sit down to make a pros-and-cons list about leaving your job, your brain isn't asking "what will make me come alive?" It's asking "how do I avoid getting eaten by a saber-toothed tiger?" That survival mechanism is 100,000 years old, and it's running the show every time you try to make a career change.

So you get a list that looks like this:

  • Pros of staying: steady paycheck, health insurance, known quantity, people know me here
  • Cons of leaving: uncertainty, financial risk, starting over, what if I fail

Every single item on that list is fear-based. Every single one is your brain trying to keep you safe in a cave. But you're not in a cave. You're in a career that's slowly draining your will to live. And the pros-and-cons list will never, ever tell you that. Because the pros-and-cons list can't measure the cost of staying. It can't measure the slow erosion of your spirit. It can't measure the way you feel smaller every year. It can't measure the grief of watching your potential wither on the vine while you pretend everything is fine.

Your intuition isn't telling you what to do. It's telling you what's true. And what's true is that staying is costing you more than leaving ever could.

Does that land? Because I need you to really sit with that. Your rational brain is running a cost-benefit analysis that only counts visible costs. It doesn't count invisible costs. It doesn't count the cost of your aliveness. It doesn't count the cost of your creativity. It doesn't count the cost of waking up every morning with a sense of dread that you've normalized so completely you don't even notice it anymore.

The Body Knows Before You Do

Here's a practice I want you to try. It's simple. It's going to feel weird. But it works. Close your eyes and bring to mind your current job. Just the thought of it. The building. The desk. The meetings. The people. Notice what happens in your body. Do your shoulders tighten? Does your jaw clench? Does your stomach get tight? Does your breath get shallow?

Now bring to mind the thing you actually want to do. The career you've been dreaming about but have been too scared to pursue. The thing you were doing when you were ten years old and no one had told you yet that you couldn't. Notice what happens in your body. Does your chest relax? Do you feel a warmth? Does your breath get deeper? Do you feel a sense of expansion?

Your body is giving you data right now. This isn't woo-woo. This is your autonomic nervous system responding to two different environments. Your body knows which one is safe for your soul. Your body knows which one is killing you. The problem is you've been trained to override this data with your rational mind. You've been trained to think that the tightness in your chest is just anxiety about change, when really it's anxiety about not changing.

I want you to get a journal or a notebook - something physical, not a phone note. Start tracking these body responses for one week. Every time you think about your current job, write down what you feel in your body. Every time you think about the change you want to make, write down what you feel. By the end of the week, you'll have a physical record of what your intuition has been telling you all along. You'll see it in black and white. And you won't be able to unsee it.

The Fear of Wasting Your Potential

There's this fear that comes up a lot when people are considering a career change. It's not just fear of failure. It's fear of wasting your potential. What if you leave and the new thing doesn't work out? What if you've been telling yourself you're meant for more, but actually you're not? What if you're just average and this is as good as it gets?

I want to name something here. That voice - the one that says you're not good enough, that you're being arrogant to think you could do something else, that you should just be grateful for what you have - that voice is not your intuition. That voice is your conditioning. That voice is every teacher who told you to be realistic. Every parent who told you to have a backup plan. Every boss who made you feel replaceable. That voice is the voice of a world that wants you small and manageable.

Your intuition doesn't speak in fear. Your intuition speaks in clarity. It doesn't say "you're not good enough." It says "this isn't right for you." It doesn't say "you'll fail." It says "there's something better." It doesn't say "be realistic." It says "you already know what you need to do."

If you're struggling to distinguish between fear and intuition, here's a simple test. Fear feels tight, contracted, and urgent. It demands an answer now. It keeps you up at night spinning in circles. Intuition feels calm, clear, and patient. It doesn't need you to decide today. It just keeps showing up, quietly, persistently, until you're ready to listen.

There's a book I recommend to almost everyone I work with who's going through a career transition. It's called The Artist's Way by Julia Cameron. Now, I know the title says "artist" and you might not think of yourself as an artist. But if you're considering a career change, you're an artist of your own life. You're trying to create something new. And this book has a practice called Morning Pages that is absolutely essential for clearing the noise and hearing your intuition. See on Amazon (paid link)

The practice is simple. Every morning, you write three pages by hand. Stream of consciousness. No editing. No censoring. Just whatever comes out. You'll write a lot of garbage. You'll write about what you're angry about, what you're scared of, what you're resentful about. And then, buried in all that garbage, you'll find gold. You'll find a sentence that stops you cold. You'll find a piece of clarity that you didn't know you had. That's your intuition breaking through the noise.

The Practical Side of Trusting Yourself

Okay, let's get practical. Because I don't want you to quit your job tomorrow and end up in a worse situation. That's not intuition - that's impulsivity. There's a difference. Intuition doesn't tell you to burn everything down. It tells you to start building something new while you still have a foundation.

Here's a approach I use with my students. It's not complicated. It's three questions you ask yourself before making any career decision:

  • Does this feel expansive or contractive in my body?
  • Does this align with what I know to be true about myself, even if it's scary?
  • Am I making this decision from fear or from clarity?

If the answer to the first two is "expansive" and "yes," and the answer to the third is "clarity," you're probably on the right track. If you're getting "contractive," "no," and "fear," you need to slow down. Not stop. Slow down. Because sometimes fear and intuition look the same at first. You need time to sit with them and let them reveal themselves.

I also want you to start experimenting. You don't have to quit your job to test a new career. You can start small. Take a class. Do a side project. Talk to someone who's doing what you want to do. Volunteer. The goal isn't to have all the answers. The goal is to gather data. Your intuition works best when it has real information to work with. When you're sitting in your car imagining what it would be like to do something else, your intuition is guessing. When you've actually done a few hours of that work, your intuition has something to work with.

There's another book that's been incredibly helpful for people in this phase. It's Playing Big by Tara Mohr. She talks about something called the "inner critic" and how it specifically shows up when you're about to make a big change. Her work is grounded, practical, and deeply compassionate. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Grief of Leaving

Here's something no one talks about when it comes to career changes. There's grief. Even when the change is right. Even when you're leaving something that was killing you. There's grief for the person you were. There's grief for the time you spent. There's grief for the relationships you built. There's grief for the identity you're shedding.

I had a student who left a fifteen-year career in corporate law to become a world designer. She knew it was right. Her body told her. Her dreams told her. Every sign pointed toward yes. And still, the week after she gave her notice, she sobbed in my office for an hour. Not because she was making a mistake. Because she was grieving. She was grieving the version of herself that had worked so hard to become a lawyer. She was grieving the respect she got at dinner parties when she said what she did for a living. She was grieving the certainty of a known identity.

Your intuition will lead you to the right place. But it won't protect you from the grief of leaving. And you need to let yourself feel that grief. You need to honor it. Because if you don't, it will turn into resentment. It will turn into second-guessing. It will turn into a voice that says "see, I told you this was a mistake" the first time the new path gets hard.

So let yourself grieve. Write a letter to your old career. Thank it for what it gave you. Acknowledge what it cost you. And then let it go. This isn't weakness. This is wisdom. This is the work of becoming who you actually are.

When the Path Gets Quiet

There will be a phase in your career change where everything goes quiet. You've made the decision. You've taken the leap. And now you're in the in-between. The old job is gone. The new identity hasn't fully formed yet. You're in the void. And the void is terrifying.

Your intuition will feel silent during this phase. Not because it's gone. Because it's working. It's doing its deep, underground work. Like a seed in the soil. You can't see it growing. You can't see the roots spreading. But something is happening. Something is preparing to break through the surface.

This is the most important time to stay grounded. Don't panic. Don't go back to the old thing just because the new thing is taking longer than you expected. Don't take the first job that comes along just to feel safe again. Stay in the discomfort. Trust the process. Your intuition didn't lead you here to abandon you. It led you here because you needed to be here.

I recommend Letting Go by David R. Hawkins during this phase. It's a dense book, but it's incredibly helpful for understanding how to release the fear and resistance that comes up when you're in transition. See on Amazon (paid link)

You Already Know

Here's the truth I want to leave you with. You already know what to do. You've always known. The reason you're reading this article isn't because you need more information. It's because you need permission. You need someone to tell you that it's okay to trust yourself. That it's okay to want something different. That it's okay to be scared and do it anyway.

So here it is. Permission granted. You're allowed to leave. You're allowed to want more. You're allowed to trust the feeling in your body that's been telling you for years that you're in the wrong place. You're allowed to disappoint people. You're allowed to start over. You're allowed to be a beginner again. You're allowed to become the person you actually are, not the person you were told to be.

Your intuition is not broken. It's been trying to get your attention for a long time. It's been sending you signals through your body, through your dreams, through the books you're drawn to, through the people you admire, through the quiet voice that says "what if." You've been ignoring it because you were taught to. But you don't have to keep ignoring it.

Start small. Start today. Sit in the parking lot for eleven more minutes. But this time, instead of pushing through, listen. Ask yourself one question: "If I trusted myself completely, what would I do?" And then wait for the answer. It's already there. It's been there all along.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've created a short assessment that helps you identify where you are in your intuitive development and what's blocking you from trusting yourself. It takes about ten minutes and it'll give you a clear picture of what's actually going on beneath the surface. Take the assessment here.
